title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the four texts about the history and future of work. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the authors (A – D).

content.title: The Daily Grind: Past, Present, and Future

texts.A:
### The Sociologist (Dr. Lane)
Dr. Lane examines how work defines identity. [47]She points out that for most of human history, 'work' was indistinguishable from daily survival, and the concept of a 'career' is a very recent invention.[/47] [51]She highlights that the industrial era introduced the idea of the 'clock' as our master, replacing the natural rhythms of the sun.[/51] [54]She warns that the rise of the 'gig economy' is returning us to a pre-industrial state of insecurity, where workers have no predictable income.[/54] [56]She argues that we need to decouple our self-worth from our job titles, as automation will soon make many roles obsolete.[/56]
texts.B:
### The Economist (Marcus Vane)
Marcus Vane is interested in productivity and wealth. [48]He notes that despite technological advances, the average work week hasn't shrunk as predicted in the 1930s.[/48] [52]He argues that we have created 'bullshit jobs'—roles that serve no real purpose but exist to keep the economy moving.[/52] [50]He mentions that the shift toward 'knowledge work' has made it harder to measure actual value, leading to a culture of 'performative busyness'.[/50] [53]However, he highlights that remote work has finally started to break the link between where you live and what you earn.[/53]
texts.C:
### The Historian (Sarah)
Sarah looks back at the medieval period. [49]She surprises many by pointing out that a medieval peasant actually had more days off than a modern office worker[/49], thanks to the frequent religious festivals. [55]She notes that work was once a collective activity, whereas today it is increasingly solitary and competitive.[/55] [51]She describes how the 'Protestant work ethic' transformed work from a necessary evil into a moral duty.[/51] [47]For her, the current obsession with 'hustle culture' is a direct descendant of these 17th-century religious ideas.[/47]
texts.D:
### The Futurist (Kevin)
Kevin believes the 'four-day work week' is inevitable. [50]He highlights companies that have already made the switch and seen a massive spike in employee retention and health.[/50] [52]He argues that our current 40-hour model is a relic of the factory era that has no place in a creative economy.[/52] [56]He suggests that a 'Universal Basic Income' will be necessary to support a society where robots do the manual labor.[/56] [53]He admits that the biggest barrier to change is not technology, but the 'work-as-suffering' mindset of current leadership.[/53] [48]He points out that leisure time will become the new status symbol.[/48]

questions.question: trace the origins of a modern cultural trend back to centuries-old beliefs?
questions.explanation: Sarah traces 'hustle culture' back to 17th-century religious ideas.

questions.question: point out that a specific historical prediction about free time has failed to come true?
questions.explanation: Marcus Vane notes the work week hasn't shrunk as predicted in the 1930s.

questions.question: provide a comparison that contradicts the common view that modern life is more relaxed than the past?
questions.explanation: Sarah says medieval peasants had more days off than modern office workers.

questions.question: mention that certain modern work cultures prioritize the appearance of effort over actual results?
questions.explanation: Marcus Vane mentions 'performative busyness' in knowledge work.

questions.question: refer to a time when humans were forced to adapt to a non-natural way of measuring time?
questions.explanation: Dr. Lane mentions the 'clock' replacing the natural rhythms of the sun.

questions.question: claim that many contemporary job roles are essentially unnecessary?
questions.explanation: Marcus Vane argues that we have created 'bullshit jobs' with no real purpose.

questions.question: identify a psychological rather than a technical hurdle to workplace reform?
questions.explanation: Kevin says the barrier is the 'work-as-suffering' mindset of leadership.

questions.question: mention a modern employment trend that shares characteristics with the era before factories existed?
questions.explanation: Dr. Lane says the 'gig economy' returns us to pre-industrial insecurity.

questions.question: note a shift from communal working environments to more individualistic ones?
questions.explanation: Sarah notes work was once collective but is now solitary and competitive.

questions.question: suggest that we need to redefine how we value ourselves as human beings?
questions.explanation: Dr. Lane argues we need to decouple self-worth from job titles.
